content.assessment: This proposal leaves all major institutional mechanisms fully intact: centralized entrance exams (F-live-hungarian_education_system-03), the 85% school-selection capacity that dwarfs the OECD average (F-live-hungarian_education_system-01), and maintainer exemptions permitting segregated schooling (F-live-hungarian_education_system-05). From an institutional-fit perspective it requires zero administrative adaptation, but system coverage of the equity problem is nil since no lever identified in the evidence base is touched. The proposal explicitly monitors rather than addresses documented outcomes: Hungary's extreme segregation (F-live-hungarian_education_system-02) and below-average disadvantaged proficiency (F-live-hungarian_education_system-07) would continue on their current trajectory. Unintended sorting mechanisms, including geographic settlement effects (F-live-hungarian_education_system-06), remain wholly unaddressed.
opportunities: Could serve as a baseline against which other proposals' institutional effects are measured
strengths: Maximal institutional compatibility since no existing admissions, governance, or maintainer structures are altered
Leverages already-existing PISA/ABC monitoring infrastructure without new administrative build
threats: Continued high selection intensity (85% vs 43% OECD) combined with extreme socioeconomic segregation risks further entrenchment of stratified access with no institutional counter-pressure
weaknesses: Does not engage with any of the specific selection, exemption, or segregation mechanisms documented in the evidence
Monitoring without correction history means measured segregation and proficiency gaps are highly likely to persist unchanged
